quote.text: In every walk with nature, one receives far more than he seeks.
quote.author: John Muir

fun_fact.text: The Strait of Juan de Fuca, which Barbara can see from Port Angeles, was named after a Greek navigator who sailed for Spain in 1592. He claimed to have found a passage between the Pacific and Atlantic oceans, which turned out to be this beautiful waterway separating Washington from Vancouver Island!

short_read.title: The Secret Language of Flowers in Victorian Times
short_read.teaser: In the 1800s, people sent messages through carefully chosen flower bouquets—each bloom had a specific meaning. Red roses meant love, but yellow ones signaled friendship, and forget-me-nots meant exactly what their name suggests!

medium_read.title: The Remarkable Intelligence of Crows
medium_read.teaser: Scientists have discovered that crows can recognize human faces, use tools, and even hold grudges for years. These clever birds, common in the Pacific Northwest, have memories and problem-solving skills that rival some primates.

long_read.title: Olympic National Park: A Wilderness Like No Other
long_read.teaser: From temperate rainforests to glacier-capped mountains, Olympic National Park contains an incredible diversity of ecosystems all within Barbara's backyard. This exploration reveals how this unique corner of Washington became one of America's most treasured wild places.

port_angeles.title: Black-Tailed Deer of the Olympic Peninsula
port_angeles.description: The Columbian black-tailed deer are a common and delightful sight around Port Angeles. These gentle creatures are smaller than their white-tailed cousins and are perfectly adapted to the mild, wet climate of the Pacific Northwest. Early morning or dusk are the best times to spot them grazing near the forest edges.

puzzle_1.question: A hummingbird's heart can beat up to ___ times per minute while flying.
puzzle_1.correct_response: Exactly right, Barbara! That tiny heart works incredibly hard to power those amazing wings!
puzzle_1.close_response: You're very close! The answer is 1,200 beats per minute—that's about 20 times per second!
puzzle_1.wrong_response: Those tiny hearts are even faster than you might think—up to 1,200 beats per minute! It's hard to imagine something so small working so hard.
puzzle_1.learn_more_text: Hummingbirds have the highest metabolism of all animals—they must eat about half their body weight in sugar daily just to survive. Their hearts slow down to about 250 beats per minute when they sleep.

puzzle_2.question: Which Hungarian word means 'hello' or 'goodbye'?
puzzle_2.correct_response: Wonderful, Barbara! Szia is such a friendly, versatile word—perfect for both coming and going!
puzzle_2.wrong_response: The word is 'Szia'—it's one of those lovely informal words that works for both hello and goodbye, just like 'ciao' in Italian!
puzzle_2.learn_more_text: Szia comes from the Hungarian word 'szolga' meaning servant, originally from the phrase 'your servant.' It's the informal way to greet friends, while 'Viszlát' is specifically for goodbye.

puzzle_3.question: What special adaptation allows seals to sleep underwater?
options: They can sleep with half their brain at a time
They hold their breath for hours
puzzle_3.correct_response: Perfect! Seals can literally sleep with one eye open, resting half their brain while the other half stays alert. Nature is amazing!
puzzle_3.wrong_response: Seals have a wonderful trick—they can sleep with half their brain at a time! One hemisphere rests while the other keeps them aware enough to surface for air.
puzzle_3.learn_more_text: This ability, called unihemispheric sleep, allows seals to rest while still surfacing to breathe and watching for predators. The harbor seals you might see in the Strait of Juan de Fuca use this remarkable adaptation every day.

game.title: Bubble Shooter
game.description: A relaxing bubble puzzle game — aim and pop matching colored bubbles. Easy to pick up, fun to play!
